content.text: The Observe Step in ReAct
After executing an action, the agent reads the result — this is the
Observation
. In LLM agent systems, observations are the
tool_result

blocks returned by the host application and appended to the message history. The model reads these in the next iteration's context window and uses them to update its beliefs about the environment before writing the next Thought.

Observations are the agent's primary feedback loop. Without them, the agent is planning in a vacuum — it cannot tell whether its last action succeeded, what the data actually says, or whether the environment has changed. The quality of an observation determines the quality of the next reasoning step.
Anatomy of a Tool Result
Every observation in the Anthropic API is a
tool_result
message: a user-role message containing one or more blocks, each matched to a prior
tool_use
block by its
tool_use_id

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def search(query: str) -> str:
    # Stub — replace with a real search API
    return json.dumps({
        "query": query,
        "results": [
            {"title": "Paris overview", "snippet": "Paris is the capital of France with ~2.1M residents."},
            {"title": "Paris tourism",  "snippet": "Paris attracts 30M visitors per year."},
        ],
        "total_results": 2,
    })

TOOLS = [{
    "name": "search",
    "description": "Search for information.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}},
        "required": ["query"],
    },
}]

messages = [{"role": "user", "content": "What is the population of Paris?"}]

# Round 1 — model calls the tool
resp = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=512,
    tools=TOOLS,
    messages=messages,
)
messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content})

# Build the observation (tool_result)
tool_results = []
for block in resp.content:
    if block.type == "tool_use":
        result = search(**block.input)
        tool_results.append({
            "type": "tool_result",
            "tool_use_id": block.id,   # MUST match the tool_use block id
            "content": result,          # string (JSON, plain text, or XML)
        })

# Append the observation — this is the "Observe" step
messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

# Round 2 — model reads the observation and reasons about it
resp2 = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=512,
    tools=TOOLS,
    messages=messages,
)
print(resp2.content[0].text)
The
tool_use_id
field is critical. If it does not match the
id
from the original
tool_use
block, the API will reject the message. When dispatching multiple tool calls in parallel, each result must carry the correct ID.
Observation Quality: What Makes a Good Result

The model's next Thought is only as good as the observation it reads. A good observation is structured, concise, and contains exactly the information the model needs. A poor observation forces the model to parse noise, misread data, or make assumptions.
Observation quality
Example
Effect on reasoning
Model extracts exact values; accurate reasoning
"The product costs about a hundred dollars and has some stock"
Model must parse; may misread numbers
Full 10,000-line API response
Model skims; key fields may be missed; context fills faster
{"ok": false, "error": "not found"}
Model recognises failure; can try alternative tool
Traceback (most recent call last): ...
Model confused; wastes tokens; may hallucinate a fix
{"results": []}
Model correctly infers no data found; can try different query
Structure every observation as JSON. Return
"ok": true
or
"ok": false
at the top level so the model can detect success or failure immediately. Return only the fields the model actually needs — trim large API responses before returning them.
Handling Empty and Unexpected Observations
Not every tool call returns useful data. The model must be prepared to handle three failure modes:
Empty results
: the query returned nothing (e.g. search with no matches). The model should try a broader or alternative query, not give up.
Error results
: the tool call failed (rate limit, not found, network error). The model should read the error, decide whether to retry or try an alternative tool.
Unexpected format

: the result is valid but not what was expected (a list when a dict was expected, a string where a number was required). The model should describe what it received and ask the caller to clarify if needed.
Observation Size and Context Management

Each observation appended to the message history increases the input token count for every subsequent API call. Large observations — full API responses, long documents, verbose log files — are the most common cause of runaway context growth in agentic loops.
Apply observation trimming at the dispatcher level, before the result enters the message history:

Keep observations under 2,000 characters wherever possible. A well-trimmed observation that contains the key facts is more useful to the model than an un-trimmed observation that buries them in noise.
Accumulating Observations Across Iterations

Each iteration adds new observations to the context. After several rounds, the context contains a growing log of Thought / Action / Observation triples. The model reads this full history at each step, which is useful (the model sees its own reasoning trace) but costly (input tokens grow with each round).
When the context is growing too large, two strategies help:
Observation summarisation
: after several rounds, inject a one-sentence summary of each past observation and remove the full observation text. The model loses verbatim detail but retains the key facts.
Rolling window
: keep only the last N Thought/Action/Observation triples verbatim. Earlier triples are either dropped or replaced with a compressed summary.
Parallel Observations
When the model emits multiple tool calls in a single response, the host dispatches them concurrently and returns all results in a single tool_results message. From the model's perspective, it sees
multiple observations at once
in the next round. This is efficient but requires the model to process and integrate several results simultaneously.

import anthropic
import json
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def get_stock_price(ticker: str) -> str:
    prices = {"AAPL": 182.50, "GOOG": 141.30, "MSFT": 378.90}
    price = prices.get(ticker.upper())
    if price is None:
        return json.dumps({"ok": False, "error": f"Unknown ticker: {ticker}"})
    return json.dumps({"ok": True, "ticker": ticker.upper(), "price": price})

TOOLS = [{
    "name": "get_stock_price",
    "description": "Get current stock price for a ticker symbol.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {"ticker": {"type": "string", "description": "Stock ticker symbol e.g. AAPL"}},
        "required": ["ticker"],
    },
}]

messages = [
    {"role": "user", "content": "Compare the current prices of Apple, Google, and Microsoft."},
]

resp = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=512,
    tools=TOOLS,
    messages=messages,
)
messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content})

tool_calls = [b for b in resp.content if b.type == "tool_use"]

# Execute all tool calls in parallel and collect observations
def execute(block):
    result = get_stock_price(**block.input)
    return {
        "type": "tool_result",
        "tool_use_id": block.id,
        "content": result,
    }

with ThreadPoolExecutor() as ex:
    observations = list(ex.map(execute, tool_calls))

# All three observations arrive in one "user" message
messages.append({"role": "user", "content": observations})

resp2 = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=512,
    tools=TOOLS,
    messages=messages,
)
print(resp2.content[0].text)
# "Apple is trading at $182.50, Google at $141.30, and Microsoft at $378.90.
#  Microsoft is the most expensive of the three."

When multiple observations arrive together, the model integrates them all before writing the next Thought. This is more efficient than sequential calls: three lookups in one round instead of three sequential rounds, with the same total number of API calls.
Observation-Driven Replanning

An observation may invalidate the agent's original plan. If the model planned to book flight A but the observation shows A is sold out, the Thought in the next round must incorporate this new information and replan. This is
observation-driven replanning
— the core of adaptive agent behavior.
Observation Freshness and Caching
Observations become
stale
as time passes and the environment changes. An observation fetched at the start of a long loop may no longer reflect reality by the time the agent acts on it. Two patterns manage staleness:
Re-observe before write
: always re-fetch the relevant state immediately before executing a write action, even if the same data was fetched earlier in the same session.
Timestamp observations
: include the fetch time in each observation so the next Thought can reference how old the data is:

Caching read-only observations for re-use within the same session is safe as long as the TTL is short relative to how fast the environment changes. A product price that changes weekly can be cached for a session. A stock quantity that changes in seconds should never be cached.
Observations are the ground truth the agent reasons from.
A raw exception or an empty string gives the model nothing to work with. A structured JSON observation with an explicit
ok

flag, the relevant fields, and a consistent shape lets the model detect success, identify errors, and plan the next step accurately. Normalise and trim observations before they enter the context — they are the highest-leverage point for controlling both accuracy and token cost in a ReAct loop.
